content.assessment: A codified presumption-against-closure duty is politically attractive because it can be attached to the already-scheduled 2026 governance reform, but it operates against a structural reality: since 2013 closure-relevant authority sits with a central bureaucracy (KLIK) that is largely insulated from village-level electoral accountability, and reversals of closure decisions to date have occurred only through ad hoc protest-driven intervention rather than codified rights. This means the proposal's success depends on whether central authorities are willing to cede genuine veto power rather than symbolic procedure, which is a coalition question distinct from the procedural design itself.
opportunities: Nationwide teacher/parent mobilization (2022-2023) demonstrates an organized coalition that could be mobilized to support statutory codification as a stable, enforceable analogue to protest-based reversal
strengths: Attaches to an existing legislative vehicle (2026 reform) rather than requiring a freestanding political mobilization
Builds on a demonstrated precedent that concentrated protest can reverse centrally-made closure decisions, suggesting public legitimacy demand exists for stronger safeguards
threats: Central administrators have institutional incentive to resist ceding discretion, since the 2013 reform was itself designed to remove local decision authority
Without an independent oversight body, application risk becoming inconsistent or captured by the same central agency it is meant to constrain
weaknesses: Centralized ownership of the maintenance function since 2013 means any statutory duty could be nominal unless it transfers real halting authority away from the insulated central agency
No existing institutional culture of codified appeal to build on; current reversals are informal and case-specific
